{{short description|Behaviour in which an individual observes and replicates another's behaviour}}
{{About|the behaviour in humans and animals}}
{{more citations needed|date=October 2018}}
{{Use mdy dates|date=February 2014}}
[[File:Walter and Seth Singleton.jpg|alt=|thumb|300x300px|A toddler imitates his father.]]
'''Imitation''' (from Latin ''imitatio'', "a copying, imitation"<ref>[http://www.etymonline.com/index.php?term=imitation imitation], Online etymology dictionary</ref>) is a [[behavior]] whereby an individual observes and replicates another's behavior. Imitation is also a form of learning that leads to the "development of [[tradition]]s, and ultimately our [[culture]]. It allows for the transfer of [[information]] (behaviors, customs, etc.) between individuals and down generations without the need for [[genetic inheritance]]."<ref name="Hopper, L. M. 2010">{{cite journal |last=Hopper |first=Lydia M. |year=2010 |title=Deferred imitation in children and apes |journal=Psychologist |volume=23 |issue=4 |pages=294–7 |url=https://thepsychologist.bps.org.uk/volume-23/edition-4/deferred-imitation-children-and-apes |access-date=March 8, 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140803200853/http://www.thepsychologist.org.uk/archive/archive_home.cfm?volumeID=23&editionID=187&ArticleID=1657 |archive-date=August 3, 2014 }}</ref> The word ''imitation'' can be applied in many contexts, ranging from [[animal training]] to [[politics]].<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/026218 |title=Imitation is the Greatest form of Flattery |year=1988 |last1=Moss |first1=Ellen |last2=Strayer |first2=F. F. |journal=PsycCRITIQUES |volume=33 |issue=11|pages=970–971 }}</ref> The term generally refers to [[Consciousness|conscious]] behavior; [[subconscious]] imitation is termed [[Mirroring (psychology)|mirroring]].<ref name="Chartrand & Bargh">{{cite journal|last1=Chartrand|first1=T.|last2=Bargh|first2=J.|title=The Chameleon Effect: The Perception-Behavior Link and Social Interaction|url=http://www.yale.edu/acmelab/articles/chartrand_bargh_1999.pdf|journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology|year=1999|volume=76|issue=6|pages=893–910|publisher=New York University|doi=10.1037/0022-3514.76.6.893|pmid=10402679|bibcode=1999JPSP...76..893C |s2cid=11818459|access-date=2014-09-28|ref=3|archive-date=May 29, 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150529021022/http://www.yale.edu/acmelab/articles/chartrand_bargh_1999.pdf}}</ref>

==Anthropology and social sciences==
In [[anthropology]], some [[Theory|theories]] hold that all cultures imitate [[idea]]s from one of a few original cultures or several cultures whose influence overlaps geographically. Evolutionary diffusion theory holds that cultures influence one another, but that similar ideas can be developed in isolation.

Scholars<ref>[[George Herbert Mead]], ''Mind, Self, and Society''; [[Kenneth Kaye]], ''[[The Mental and Social Life of Babies]]'', U. Chicago Press 1982{{Page needed|date=April 2013}}</ref> as well as popular authors<ref>[[Susan Blackmore]], ''The Meme Machine'', Oxford U. Press 2007{{Page needed|date=April 2013}}</ref><ref>Jared Diamond, ''[[The Third Chimpanzee]]'', HarperCollins 1992.{{Page needed|date=April 2013}}</ref> have argued that the role of imitation in [[human]]s is unique among [[animal]]s. However, this claim has been recently challenged by scientific research which observed [[Social learning in animals|social learning]] and [[#Imitation in animals|imitative abilities in animals]].

Psychologist [[Kenneth Kaye]] showed<ref>{{cite book|editor-first=Margaret|editor-last=Bullowa|title=Before Speech: The beginning of interpersonal communication|publisher=Cambridge U. Press|year=1979|isbn=0521220319}}{{Page needed|date=April 2013}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/0012-1649.17.3.258 |title=Infant imitation: The sensory-motor agenda |year=1981 |last1=Kaye |first1=Kenneth |last2=Marcus |first2=Janet |journal=Developmental Psychology |volume=17 |issue=3 |page=258}}</ref> that the ability of [[infant]]s to match the sounds or [[gesture]]s of an [[adult]] depends on an interactive process of [[turn-taking]] over many successive trials, in which adults' instinctive behavior plays as great a role as that of the infant. These writers assume that evolution would have selected imitative abilities as fit because those who were good at it had a wider arsenal of learned behavior at their disposal, including tool-making and [[language]].

However, research also suggests that imitative behaviors and other [[Social learning theory|social learning]] processes are only selected for when outnumbered or accompanied by asocial learning processes: an over-saturation of imitation and imitating individuals leads humans to collectively copy inefficient [[Strategy|strategies]] and evolutionarily [[Maladaptation|maladaptive]] behaviors, thereby reducing flexibility to new environmental contexts that require [[adaptation]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Rendell|first1=Luke|last2=Fogarty|first2=Laurel|last3=Laland|first3=Kevin N.|date=2010|title=Rogers' Paradox Recast and Resolved: Population Structure and the Evolution of Social Learning Strategies|url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/j.1558-5646.2009.00817.x|journal=Evolution|language=en|volume=64|issue=2|pages=534–548|doi=10.1111/j.1558-5646.2009.00817.x|pmid=19674093|bibcode=2010Evolu..64..534R |s2cid=2898779|issn=1558-5646|url-access=subscription}}</ref> Research suggests imitative social learning hinders the acquisition of [[knowledge]] in novel environments and in situations where asocial learning is faster and more advantageous.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Laland|first1=Kevin N.|last2=Williams|first2=Kerry|date=1998|title=Social transmission of maladaptive information in the guppy|journal=Behavioral Ecology|volume=9|issue=5|pages=493–499|doi=10.1093/beheco/9.5.493|issn=1045-2249|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Johnstone|first1=R. A.|last2=Dall|first2=S. R. X.|last3=Giraldeau|first3=Luc–Alain|last4=Valone|first4=Thomas J.|last5=Templeton|first5=Jennifer J.|date=2002-11-29|title=Potential disadvantages of using socially acquired information|journal=Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society of London. Series B: Biological Sciences|volume=357|issue=1427|pages=1559–1566|doi=10.1098/rstb.2002.1065|pmc=1693065|pmid=12495513}}</ref>

In the mid-20th century, social scientists began to study how and why people imitate ideas. [[Everett Rogers]] pioneered [[innovation diffusion]] studies, identifying factors in adoption and profiles of adopters of ideas.<ref>Rogers, EM (2010). "Diffusion of innovations". Simon and Schuster.</ref> Imitation mechanisms play a central role in both analytical and empirical models of collective human behavior.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Castellano |first1=Claudio |last2=Fortunato |first2=Santo |last3=Loreto |first3=Vittorio |title=Statistical physics of social dynamics |journal=Reviews of Modern Physics |date=11 May 2009 |volume=81 |issue=2 |pages=591–646 |doi=10.1103/RevModPhys.81.591 |arxiv=0710.3256 |bibcode=2009RvMP...81..591C |s2cid=118376889 }}</ref>

==[[Neuroscience]]==
Humans are capable of imitating movements, actions, [[skill]]s, behaviors, gestures, pantomimes, mimics, vocalizations, sounds, speech, etc. and that we have particular "imitation systems" in the brain is old neurological knowledge dating back to [[Hugo Karl Liepmann]]. Liepmann's model 1908 "''Das hierarchische Modell der Handlungsplanung''" (the hierarchical model of action planning) is still valid. On studying the cerebral localization of function, Liepmann postulated that planned or commanded actions were prepared in the parietal lobe of the brain's dominant hemisphere, and also frontally. His most important pioneering work is when extensively studying patients with lesions in these brain areas, he discovered that the patients lost (among other things) the ability to imitate. He was the one who coined the term "[[apraxia]]" and differentiated between [[Ideational apraxia|ideational]] and [[Ideomotor apraxia|ideomotor]] apraxia. It is in this basic and wider frame of classical neurological knowledge that the discovery of the [[mirror neuron]] has to be seen. Though mirror neurons were first discovered in macaques, their discovery also relates to humans.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Bonini |first1=Luca |last2=Ferrari |first2=Pier Francesco |title=Evolution of mirror systems: a simple mechanism for complex cognitive functions |journal=Annals of the New York Academy of Sciences |date=2011 |volume=1225 |issue=1 |pages=166–175 |doi=10.1111/j.1749-6632.2011.06002.x |pmc=3113469 |pmid=21535003 |bibcode=2011NYASA1225..166B }}</ref>

Human brain studies using [[functional magnetic resonance imaging]] (fMRI) revealed a network of regions in the inferior [[frontal cortex]] and inferior [[parietal cortex]] which are typically activated during imitation tasks.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1126/science.286.5449.2526 |title=Cortical Mechanisms of Human Imitation |year=1999 |last1=Iacoboni |first1=M. |journal=Science |volume=286 |issue=5449 |pages=2526–8 |pmid=10617472 |last2=Woods |first2=RP |last3=Brass |first3=M |last4=Bekkering |first4=H |last5=Mazziotta |first5=JC |last6=Rizzolatti |first6=G|citeseerx=10.1.1.555.8075 }}</ref> It has been suggested that these regions contain [[mirror neurons]] similar to the mirror neurons recorded in the [[macaque monkey]].<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1146/annurev.neuro.27.070203.144230 |title=The Mirror-Neuron System |year=2004 |last1=Rizzolatti |first1=Giacomo |last2=Craighero |first2=Laila |journal=Annual Review of Neuroscience |volume=27 |pages=169–92 |pmid=15217330|s2cid=1729870 }}</ref> However, it is not clear if macaques spontaneously imitate each other in the wild.

Neurologist [[V.S. Ramachandran|V. S. Ramachandran]] argues that the evolution of mirror neurons were important in the human acquisition of complex skills such as language and believes the discovery of mirror neurons to be a most important advance in [[neuroscience]].<ref>{{cite web |first=V.S. |last=Ramachandran |title=Mirror Neurons and imitation learning as the driving force behind 'the great leap forward' in human evolution |publisher=Edge Foundation |url=http://www.edge.org/3rd_culture/ramachandran/ramachandran_index.html}}</ref> However, little evidence directly supports the theory that mirror neuron activity is involved in [[cognitive]] functions such as [[empathy]] or learning by imitation.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/j.cub.2007.11.004 |title=A mirror up to nature |year=2008 |last1=Dinstein |first1=Ilan |last2=Thomas |first2=Cibu |last3=Behrmann |first3=Marlene |last4=Heeger |first4=David J. |journal=Current Biology |volume=18 |pages=R13–8 |pmid=18177704 |issue=1 |pmc=2517574|bibcode=2008CBio...18..R13D }}</ref>

Evidence is accumulating that [[bottlenose dolphin]]s employ imitation to learn hunting and other skills from other dolphins.<ref name="Krutzen 2005">{{cite journal |bibcode=2005PNAS..102.8939K |title=Cultural transmission of tool use in bottlenose dolphins |last1=Krützen |first1=Michael |last2=Mann |first2=Janet |last3=Heithaus |first3=Michael R. |last4=Connor |first4=Richard C. |last5=Bejder |first5=Lars |last6=Sherwin |first6=William B. |volume=102 |year=2005 |pages=8939–43 |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |doi=10.1073/pnas.0500232102 |issue=25 |pmid=15947077 |pmc=1157020|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref name="BBC Adelaide">{{cite web |publisher=WDCS, the Whale and Dolphin Conservation Society |title=Wild dolphins teaches others tail walking tricks |url=http://www.wdcs.org.au/story_details.php?select=20 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090114040341/http://www.wdcs.org.au/story_details.php?select=20 |archive-date=2009-01-14 }}</ref>

Japanese monkeys have been seen to spontaneously begin washing potatoes after seeing humans washing them.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Boesch |first1=Christophe |last2=Tomasello |first2=Michael |title=Chimpanzee and Human Cultures |journal=Current Anthropology |date=December 1998 |volume=39 |issue=5 |pages=591–614 |doi=10.1086/204785 |bibcode=1998CurrA..39..591B |s2cid=55562574 |url=http://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/7e50/ad2b512409e2ead050fdd470317971e860b1.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190226122820/http://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/7e50/ad2b512409e2ead050fdd470317971e860b1.pdf |archive-date=2019-02-26 }}</ref>

==Mirror neuron system==
Research has been conducted to locate where in the brain specific parts and neurological systems are activated when humans imitate behaviors and actions of others, discovering a [[mirror neuron]] system. This neuron system allows a person to observe and then recreate the actions of others. Mirror neurons are premotor and parietal cells in the macaque brain that fire when the animal performs a goal directed action and when it sees others performing the same action."<ref name="Iacoboni, M. 2006">{{cite journal |doi=10.1038/nrn2024 |title=The mirror neuron system and the consequences of its dysfunction |year=2006 |last1=Iacoboni |first1=Marco |last2=Dapretto |first2=Mirella |journal=Nature Reviews Neuroscience |volume=7 |issue=12 |pages=942–51 |pmid=17115076|s2cid=9463011 }}</ref> Evidence suggests that the mirror neuron system also allows people to comprehend and understand the [[intention]]s and [[emotion]]s of others.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Geiger|first1=Alexander|last2=Bente|first2=Gary|last3=Lammers|first3=Sebastian|last4=Tepest|first4=Ralf|last5=Roth|first5=Daniel|last6=Bzdok|first6=Danilo|last7=Vogeley|first7=Kai|date=2019-11-15|title=Distinct functional roles of the mirror neuron system and the mentalizing system|url=http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1053811919306937|journal=NeuroImage|language=en|volume=202|article-number=116102|doi=10.1016/j.neuroimage.2019.116102|pmid=31446128|s2cid=201125892|issn=1053-8119|url-access=subscription}}</ref> Problems of the mirror neuron system may be correlated with the social inadequacies of [[Autism spectrum|autism]]. There have been many studies done showing that [[children]] with autism, compared with [[Neurotypical|typically-developing]] children, demonstrate reduced activity in the frontal mirror neuron system area when observing or imitating [[Facial expression|facial emotional expressions]]. Of course, the higher the severity of the disease, the lower the activity in the mirror neuron system is.<ref name="Iacoboni, M. 2006"/>

==Animal behavior==
Scientists debate whether animals can consciously imitate the unconscious incitement from ''sentinel'' animals, whether imitation is uniquely human, or whether humans do a complex version of what other animals do.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1007/s10071-006-0039-2 |title=Imitation: Definitions, evidence, and mechanisms |year=2006 |last1=Zentall |first1=Thomas R. |journal=Animal Cognition |volume=9 |issue=4 |pages=335–53 |pmid=17024510|s2cid=16183221 }}</ref><ref>{{cite book
 |last=Mead
 |first=George Herbert
 |date=2015
 |orig-date=1934
 |chapter=PART II: MIND 8. Imitation and the Origin of Language
 |editor1-last=Morris
 |editor1-first=Charles W.
 |editor2-last=Huebner
 |editor2-first=Daniel R.
 |editor3-last=Joas
 |editor3-first=Hans
 |title=Mind, Self & Society
 |location=Chicago
 |publisher=The University of Chicago Press
 |pages=58–59
 |doi=10.7208/chicago/9780226112879.001.0001
 |isbn=978-0-226-11273-2
 |quote=[T]he 'herding' instinct, if reduced down to something concrete in the action of the form itself...this may lead to a stampede in the herd. Something of that sort is involved in the so-called 'sentinel.' One animal, a little more sensitive than the others, lifts his head and starts to run away, and the other animals do tend to move with the sentinel form...[Y]ou [humans] unconsciously imitate [dialects]. The same thing is also true of various other [primate] mannerisms...That is what we call 'imitation,' and what is curious is that there is practically no indication of such behavior on the part of lower [life]forms. You can teach a sparrow to sing as a canary but you have to keep that sparrow constantly listening to a canary. It does not take place readily...in general the taking over of the processes of others is not natural to lower forms. Imitation seems to belong to the human form, where it has reached some sort of independent conscious existence.}}</ref> The current controversy is partly definitional. Thorndike uses "learning to do an act from seeing it done."<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/h0092987 |title=Animal intelligence: An experimental study of the associative processes in animals |year=1898 |last1=Thorndike |first1=Edward L. |journal=The Psychological Review: Monograph Supplements |volume=2 |issue=4 |pages=i–109|hdl=2027/njp.32101045694773 |s2cid=143644299 |url=http://archive.org/details/animalintelligen00thoruoft }}</ref> It has two major shortcomings: first, by using "seeing" it restricts imitation to the visual domain and excludes, e.g., vocal imitation and, second, it would also include mechanisms such as priming, contagious behavior and social facilitation,<ref>{{cite book |editor1-last=Heyes |editor1-first=Cecilia M. |editor2-last=Galef |editor2-first=Bennett G. |year=1996 |title=Social Learning in Animals: The Roots of Culture |location=San Diego |publisher=Academic Press |isbn=978-0-08-054131-0}}{{Page needed|date=April 2013}}</ref> which most scientist distinguish as separate forms of [[observational learning]]. Thorpe suggested defining imitation as "the copying of a novel or otherwise improbable act or utterance, or some act for which there is clearly no [[instinctive]] tendency."<ref>{{cite book |last=Thorpe |first=William Homan |year=1963 |title=Learning and Instinct in Animals |url=https://archive.org/details/learninginstinct0000thor |url-access=registration |location=London |publisher=Methuen |oclc=225309}}{{Page needed|date=April 2013}}</ref> This definition is favored by many scholars, though questions have been raised how strictly the term "novel" has to be interpreted and how exactly a performed act has to match the demonstration to count as a copy.

Hayes and Hayes (1952) used the "do-as-I-do" procedure to demonstrate the imitative abilities of their trained [[Common chimpanzee|chimpanzee]] "Viki."<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Hayes |first1=Keith J. |last2=Hayes |first2=Catherine |year=1952 |title=Imitation in a home-raised chimpanzee |journal=Journal of Comparative and Physiological Psychology |volume=45 |issue=5 |pages=450–9 |doi=10.1037/h0053609 |pmid=13000013}}</ref> Their study was repeatedly criticized for its subjective interpretations of their subjects' responses. Replications of this study<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1163/156853995X00036 |jstor=4535304 |title=Can Young Chimpanzees (Pan Troglodytes) Imitate Arbitrary Actions? Hayes & Hayes (1952) Revisited |year=1995 |last1=Custance |first1=Deborah M. |last2=Whiten |first2=Andrew |last3=Bard |first3=Kim A. |journal=Behaviour |volume=132 |issue=11 |pages=837–59}}</ref> found much lower matching degrees between subjects and models. However, imitation research focusing on the copying fidelity got new momentum from a study by Voelkl and Huber.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0000611 |title=Imitation as Faithful Copying of a Novel Technique in Marmoset Monkeys |year=2007 |editor1-last=Ferrari |editor1-first=Pier |last1=Voelkl |first1=Bernhard |last2=Huber |first2=Ludwig |journal=PLOS ONE |volume=2 |issue=7 |article-number=e611 |pmid=17622356 |pmc=1905941|bibcode = 2007PLoSO...2..611V |doi-access=free }} {{open access}}</ref> They analyzed the motion [[trajectories]] of both model and observer [[monkeys]] and found a high matching degree in their movement patterns.

Paralleling these studies, comparative psychologists provided tools or apparatuses that could be handled in different ways. Heyes<ref>{{cite journal |first1=C. M. |last1=Heyes |first2=G. R. |last2=Dawson |first3=T. |last3=Nokes |year=1992 |title=Imitation in rats: Initial responding and transfer evidence |journal=The Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology |volume=45 |issue=3 |pages=229–40 |doi=10.1080/14640749208401018 |doi-broken-date=July 12, 2025 |url=https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/14640749208401018}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |first1=C. M. |last1=Heyes |first2=G. R. |last2=Dawson |year=1990 |title=A demonstration of observational learning in rats using a bidirectional control |journal=The Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology |volume=42 |issue=1 |pages=59–71 |doi=10.1080/14640749008401871 |pmid=2326494|doi-broken-date=July 12, 2025 |url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/abs/10.1080/14640749008401871}}</ref> and co-workers reported evidence for imitation in [[rat]]s that pushed a lever in the same direction as their models, though later on they withdrew their claims due to methodological problems in their original setup.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1006/lmot.1999.1041 |title=Stimulus Enhancement: Controls for Social Facilitation and Local Enhancement |year=2000 |last1=Heyes |first1=C.M. |last2=Ray |first2=E.D. |last3=Mitchell |first3=C.J. |last4=Nokes |first4=T. |journal=Learning and Motivation |volume=31 |issue=2 |pages=83–98|citeseerx=10.1.1.520.2135 }}</ref> By trying to design a testing paradigm that is less arbitrary than pushing a lever to the left or to the right, Custance and co-workers<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/0735-7036.113.1.13 |title=Social learning of an artificial fruit task in capuchin monkeys (Cebus apella) |year=1999 |last1=Custance |first1=Deborah |last2=Whiten |first2=Andrew |last3=Fredman |first3=Tamar |journal=Journal of Comparative Psychology |volume=113 |pages=13–23}}</ref> introduced the "artificial fruit" paradigm, where a small object could be opened in different ways to retrieve food placed inside—not unlike a hard-shelled fruit. Using this paradigm, scientists reported evidence for imitation in monkeys<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1006/anbe.1996.0497 |title=Push or pull: An experimental study on imitation in marmosets |year=1997 |last1=Bugnyar |first1=Thomas |last2=Huber |first2=Ludwig |journal=Animal Behaviour |volume=54 |issue=4 |pages=817–31 |pmid=9344436|bibcode=1997AnBeh..54..817B |s2cid=40005662 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1006/anbe.2000.1457 |title=True imitation in marmosets |year=2000 |last1=Voelkl |first1=Bernhard |last2=Huber |first2=Ludwig |journal=Animal Behaviour |volume=60 |issue=2 |pages=195–202 |pmid=10973721|bibcode=2000AnBeh..60..195V |s2cid=31623649 }}</ref> and [[ape]]s.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/0735-7036.110.1.3 |title=Imitative learning of artificial fruit processing in children (Homo sapiens) and chimpanzees (Pan troglodytes) |year=1996 |last1=Whiten |first1=Andrew |last2=Custance |first2=Deborah M. |last3=Gomez |first3=Juan-Carlos |last4=Teixidor |first4=Patricia |last5=Bard |first5=Kim A. |journal=Journal of Comparative Psychology |volume=110 |pages=3–14 |pmid=8851548 |issue=1}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/0735-7036.115.3.272 |title=Imitative learning by captive western lowland gorillas (Gorilla gorilla gorilla) in a simulated food-processing task |year=2001 |last1=Stoinski |first1=Tara S. |last2=Wrate |first2=Joanna L. |last3=Ure |first3=Nicky |last4=Whiten |first4=Andrew |journal=Journal of Comparative Psychology |volume=115 |issue=3 |pages=272–81 |pmid=11594496}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.3758/BF03196005 |title=How do apes ape? |year=2004 |last1=Whiten |first1=Andrew |last2=Horner |first2=Victoria |last3=Litchfield |first3=Carla A. |last4=Marshall-Pescini |first4=Sarah |journal=Animal Learning & Behavior |volume=32 |pages=36–52 |pmid=15161139 |issue=1|doi-access=free }}</ref> There remains a problem with such tool (or apparatus) use studies: what animals might learn in such studies need not be the actual behavior patterns (i.e., the actions) that were observed. Instead they might learn about some effects in the environment (i.e., how the tool moves, or how the apparatus works).<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1111/j.1439-0310.2006.01269.x |title=Push or Pull: Imitation vs. Emulation in Great Apes and Human Children |year=2006 |author1-link=Claudio Tennie |last1=Tennie |first1=Claudio |last2=Call |first2=Josep |last3=Tomasello |first3=Michael |journal=Ethology |volume=112 |issue=12 |pages=1159–69|bibcode=2006Ethol.112.1159T |s2cid=56228726 }}</ref> This type of observational learning, which focuses on results, not actions, has been dubbed emulation (see [[Emulation (observational learning)]]).

In an article written by [[Carl Zimmer]], he looked into a study being done by Derek Lyons, focusing on human evolution, in which he studied a chimpanzee. He first started with showing the chimpanzee how to retrieve food from a box. The chimpanzee soon caught on and did exactly what the scientist just did. They wanted to see if the chimpanzee's brain functioned just like a human brain, so they replicated the experiment using 16 children, following the same procedure; once the children saw how it was done, they followed the same exact steps.<ref>{{cite news |last=Zimmer |first=Carl |title=Children Learn by Monkey See, Monkey Do. Chimps Don't |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2005/12/13/science/13essa.html |work=The New York Times |date=December 13, 2005}}</ref>

===Imitation in animals===
[[File:Blackbird imitating the reverse signal of a truck.webm|thumb|[[Turdus merula|Blackbird]] imitating the [[Back-up beeper|vehicle motion alarm]] of a local garbage truck in [[Brastad]], Sweden.]]
Imitation in animals is a study in the field of [[Observational learning|social learning]] where [[learning]] [[behavior]] is observed in animals specifically how animals learn and adapt through imitation. Ethologists can classify imitation in animals by the learning of certain behaviors from [[conspecifics]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|last1=Howard|first1=Maureen|last2=Keenen|first2=Michael|date=Spring 1993|title=Outline for a Functional Analysis of Imitation in Animals|journal=The Physiological Record|volume=43|issue=2|page=185|via=Business Source Complete}}</ref> More specifically, these behaviors are usually unique to the species and can be complex in nature and can benefit the individual's [[survival]].<ref name=":0" />

Some scientists believe true imitation is only produced by humans, arguing that simple learning though [[Visual perception|sight]] is not enough to sustain as a being who can truly imitate.<ref name="Zentall 2001">{{cite journal |doi=10.1080/019697201300001812 |title=Imitation in Animals: Evidence, Function, and Mechanisms |year=2001 |last1=Zentall |first1=Thomas R. |journal=Cybernetics and Systems |volume=32 |issue=1–2 |pages=53–96|bibcode=2001CySys..32...53Z |citeseerx=10.1.1.459.375 |s2cid=30219876 }}</ref> Thorpe defines true imitation as "the copying of a novel or otherwise improbable act or utterance, or some act for which there is clearly no instinctive tendency," which is highly debated for its portrayal of imitation as a mindless repeating act.<ref name="Zentall 2001"/> True imitation is produced when behavioral, [[visual]] and [[vocal]] imitation is achieved, not just the simple [[reproduction]] of exclusive behaviors.<ref name="Zentall 2001"/> Imitation is not a simple reproduction of what one sees; rather it incorporates intention and [[Motivation|purpose]].<ref name="Zentall 2001"/> Animal imitation can range from survival purpose; imitating as a function of surviving or adapting, to unknown possible [[curiosity]], which vary between different animals and produce different results depending on the measured [[intelligence]] of the animal.<ref name="Zentall 2001"/>

There is considerable evidence to support true imitation in [[animals]].<ref name="Zentall 2003 91–95">{{cite journal |doi=10.1111/1467-8721.01237 |jstor=20182847 |title=Imitation by animals: How do they do it? |year=2003 |last1=Zentall |first1=Thomas R. |journal=Current Directions in Psychological Science |volume=12 |issue=3 |pages=91–5|s2cid=145596513 }}</ref> Experiments performed on [[apes]], [[birds]] and more specifically the [[Japanese quail]] have provided positive results to imitating [[behavior]], demonstrating imitation of opaque [[behavior]].<ref name="Zentall 2003 91–95"/> However the problem that lies is in the discrepancies between what is considered true imitation in [[behavior]].<ref name="Zentall 2003 91–95"/> [[Birds]] have demonstrated [[visual]] imitation, where the [[animal]] simply does as it sees.<ref name="Zentall 2003 91–95"/> Studies on [[apes]] however have proven more advanced results in imitation, being able to remember and [[learn]] from what they [[imitate]].<ref name="Zentall 2003 91–95"/> [[Songbirds]] have specialized brain circuits for song learning and can imitate vocalizations of others. It is well established that [[Bird vocalization|birdsong]] is a type of [[animal culture]] transmitted across generations in certain groups.<ref name="Tanaka 2023"> {{cite book |last=Tanaka |first=Masashi |date=2023 |title=Acoustic Communication in Animals |chapter=Vocal Imitation, A Specialized Brain Function That Facilitates Cultural Transmission in Songbirds |publisher=Springer Singapore |pages=81–94 |doi=10.1007/978-981-99-0831-8_5 |isbn=978-981-99-0830-1}}</ref> Studies have demonstrated far more positive results with [[behavioral]] imitation in [[primates]] and [[birds]] than any other type of [[animal]].<ref name="Zentall 2003 91–95"/> Imitation in non-[[primate]] [[mammals]] and other [[animals]] have been proven difficult to conclude solid positive results for and poses a difficult question to [[scientist]]s on why that is so.<ref name="Zentall 2003 91–95"/>

====Theories====

There are two types of theories of imitation, [[Transformational Theory of Imitation|transformational]] and [[associative]]. Transformational theories suggest that the [[information]] that is required to display certain behavior is created internally through [[cognitive]] processes and observing these behaviors provides incentive to duplicate them.<ref name="Heyes 2000 215–245">{{cite book |doi=10.1016/S0065-3454(08)60106-0 |chapter=What is the Significance of Imitation in Animals? |title=Advances in the Study of Behavior |year=2000 |last1=Heyes |first1=Cecilia M. |last2=Ray |first2=Elizabeth D. |isbn=978-0-12-004529-7 |volume=29 |pages=215–45 |editor1-first=Peter J.B. |editor1-last=Slater |editor2-first=Jay S. |editor2-last=Rosenblatt |editor3-first=Charles T. |editor3-last=Snowdon |editor4-first=Timothy J. |editor4-last=Roper }}</ref> Meaning we already have the codes to recreate any behavior and observing it results in its replication. [[Albert Bandura]]'s "[[social cognitive theory]]" is one example of a transformational theory.<ref>Bandura, A. ''Social foundations of thought and action: A social cognitive theory.'' Prentice-Hall, 1986.</ref> [[Associative]], or sometimes referred to as "contiguity",<ref>Heyes, C. "Transformational and Associative Theories of Imitation". MIT Press, 2002, p. 504-505.</ref> theories suggest that the information required to display certain behaviors does not come from within ourselves but solely from our surroundings and experiences.<ref name="Heyes 2000 215–245"/> These theories have not yet provided testable predictions in the field of [[Observational learning|social learning]] in animals and have yet to conclude strong results.<ref name="Heyes 2000 215–245"/>

====New developments====

There have been three major developments in the field of animal imitation. The first, [[behavioral ecologist]]s and [[experimental psychologist]]s found there to be adaptive [[patterns]] in behaviors in different [[vertebrate]] [[species]] in biologically important situations.<ref name="Galef 1998 275–299">{{cite book |last1=Galef |first1=Bennett G. |chapter=Recent progress in studies of imitation and social learning in animals |editor1-last=Sabourin |editor1-first=Michel |editor2-last=Craik |editor2-first=Fergus |editor3-last=Robert |editor3-first=Michèle |year=1998 |title=Advances in psychological science, Vol. 2: Biological and cognitive aspects |pages=275–99 |location=Hove, England |publisher=Psychology Press |isbn=978-0-86377-471-3}}</ref> The second, [[primatologists]] and [[comparative psychologist]]s have found imperative evidence that suggest true learning through imitation in animals.<ref name="Galef 1998 275–299"/> The third, population biologists and behavioral ecologists created [[experiment]]s that demand animals to depend on [[Observational learning|social learning]] in certain manipulated [[environment (biophysical)|environments]].<ref name="Galef 1998 275–299"/>

==Child development==

Developmental psychologist [[Jean Piaget]] noted that children in a developmental phase he called the [[sensorimotor stage]] (a period which lasts up to the first two years of a child) begin to imitate observed actions.<ref name = Carlson>{{cite book |first1=Neil R. |last1=Carlson |first2=William |last2=Buskist |first3=Michael E. |last3=Enzle |first4=C Donald |last4=Heth |year=2005 |title=Psychology: The Science of Behaviour |edition=3rd |publisher=Pearson Education Canada |page=384 |isbn=978-0-205-40386-8}}</ref> This is an important stage in the development of a child because the child is beginning to think symbolically, associating behaviors with actions, thus setting the child up for the development of further symbolic thinking. [[Imitative learning]] also plays a crucial role in the development of cognitive and social communication behaviors, such as language, play, and [[joint attention]]. Imitation serves as both a learning and a social function because new skills and knowledge are acquired, and communication skills are improved by interacting in social and emotional exchanges. It is shown, however, that "children with autism exhibit significant deficits in imitation that are associated with impairments in other social communication skills."<ref name="Ingersoll, B. 2008">{{cite journal |doi=10.1097/01.IYC.0000314482.24087.14 |title=The Social Role of Imitation in Autism |year=2008 |last1=Ingersoll |first1=Brooke |journal=Infants & Young Children |volume=21 |issue=2 |pages=107–19|s2cid=10001799 |doi-access=free }}</ref> To help children with autism, reciprocal imitation training (RIT) is used. It is a naturalistic imitation intervention that helps teach the social benefits of imitation during play by increasing child responsiveness and by increasing imitative language.<ref name="Ingersoll, B. 2008"/>

[[Reinforcement learning]], both positive and negative, and [[punishment (psychology)|punishment]], are used by people that children imitate to either promote or discontinue behavior. If a child imitates a certain type of behavior or action and the consequences are rewarding, the child is very likely to continue performing the same behavior or action. The behavior "has been reinforced (i.e. strengthened)".<ref name="simplypsychology.org">{{cite web |last=McLeod |first=S. A. |year=2011 |title=Bandura: Social learning theory |publisher=Simply Psychology |access-date=November 10, 2012 |url=http://www.simplypsychology.org/bandura.html}}</ref>{{self-published inline|date=April 2013}} However, if the imitation is not accepted and approved by others, then the behavior will be weakened.

Naturally, children are surrounded by many different types of people that influence their actions and behaviors, including parents, family members, teachers, peers, and even characters on television programs. These different types of individuals that are observed are called models. According to Saul McLeod, "these models provide examples of masculine and feminine behavior to observe and imitate."<ref name="simplypsychology.org"/> Children imitate the behavior they have observed from others, regardless of the gender of the person and whether or not the behavior is gender appropriate. However, it has been proven that children will reproduce the behavior that "its society deems appropriate for its sex."<ref name="simplypsychology.org"/>

===Infants===

Infants have the ability to reveal an understanding of certain outcomes before they occur, therefore in this sense they can somewhat imitate what they have perceived. [[Andrew N. Meltzoff]], ran a series of tasks involving 14-month-old infants to imitate actions they perceived from adults. In this gathering he had concluded that the infants, before trying to reproduce the actions they wish to imitate, somehow revealed an understanding of the intended goal even though they failed to replicate the result wished to be imitated. These task implicated that the infants knew the goal intended.<ref name="Schwier, C. 2006">{{cite journal |doi=10.1207/s15327078in1003_6 |title=Rational Imitation in 12-Month-Old Infants Christiane Schwier, Catharine van Maanen, Malinda Carpenter |year=2006 |last1=Tomasello |first1=Michael |journal=Infancy |volume=10 |issue=3 |pages=303–11|s2cid=41793867 }}</ref> Gergely, Bekkering, and Király (2002) figured that infants not only understand the intended goal but also the intentions of the person they were trying to imitate engaging in "rational imitation", as described by [[Michael Tomasello|Tomasello]], [[Malinda Carpenter|Carpenter]] and others <ref name="Schwier, C. 2006"/>

It has long been claimed that newborn humans imitate bodily gestures and facial expressions as soon as their first few days of life.<ref>Meltzoff, A. N. & Moore, M. K. (1977). Imitation of facial and manual gestures by human neonates. ''Science'', ''198''(4312), 75-78.  DOI: 10.1126/science.198.4312.75</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Field|first1=T.|last2=Woodson|first2=R|last3=Greenberg|first3=R|last4=Cohen|first4=D|date=1982-10-08|title=Discrimination and imitation of facial expression by neonates|url=https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/science.7123230|journal=Science|language=en|volume=218|issue=4568|pages=179–181|doi=10.1126/science.7123230|pmid=7123230|bibcode=1982Sci...218..179F|issn=0036-8075|url-access=subscription}}</ref> For example, in a study conducted at the Mailman Centre for Child Development at the University of Miami Medical School, 74 newborn babies (with a mean age of 36 hours) were tested to see if they were able to imitate a smile, a frown and a pout, and a wide-open mouth and eyes. An observer stood behind the experimenter (so he/she couldn't see what facial expressions were being made by the experimenter) and watched only the babies' facial expressions, recording their results. Just by looking only at the babies' faces, the observer was more often able to correctly guess what facial expression was being presented to the child by the experimenter.<ref name="nytimes.com">{{cite news |date=12 October 1982 |title=Newborns found able to imitate facial expressions |work=The New York Times |access-date=November 14, 2012 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/1982/10/12/science/newborns-found-able-to-imitate-facial-expressions.html}}</ref> After the results were calculated, "the researchers concluded that...babies have an innate ability to compare an expression they see with their own sense of muscular feedback from making the movements to match that expression."<ref name="nytimes.com"/>

However, the idea that imitation is an inborn ability has been recently challenged. A research group from the University of Queensland in Australia carried out the largest-ever longitudinal study of neonatal imitation in humans. One hundred and nine newborns were shown a variety of gestures including tongue protrusion, mouth opening, happy and sad facial expressions, at four time points between one week and 9 weeks of age. The results failed to reveal compelling evidence that newborns imitate: Infants were just as likely to produce matching and non-matching gestures in response to what they saw.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Oostenbroek|first1=Janine|last2=Suddendorf|first2=Thomas|last3=Nielsen|first3=Mark|last4=Redshaw|first4=Jonathan|last5=Kennedy-Costantini|first5=Siobhan|last6=Davis|first6=Jacqueline|last7=Clark|first7=Sally|last8=Slaughter|first8=Virginia|date=May 2016|title=Comprehensive Longitudinal Study Challenges the Existence of Neonatal Imitation in Humans|journal=Current Biology|language=en|volume=26|issue=10|pages=1334–1338|doi=10.1016/j.cub.2016.03.047|pmid=27161497|s2cid=3918288|doi-access=free|bibcode=2016CBio...26.1334O }}</ref>

At around eight months, infants will start to copy their child care providers' movements when playing [[Pat-a-cake#Game|pat-a-cake]] and [[peek-a-boo]], as well as imitating familiar gestures, such as clapping hands together or patting a doll's back. At around 18 months, infants will then begin to imitate simple actions they observe adults doing, such as taking a toy phone out of a purse and saying "hello", pretending to sweep with a child-sized broom, as well as imitating using a toy hammer.{{Citation needed|date=April 2013}}

===Toddlers===

At around 30–36 months, [[toddler]]s will start to imitate their parents by pretending to get ready for work and school and saying the last word(s) of what an adult just said. For example, toddlers may say "bowl" or "a bowl" after they hear someone say, "That's a bowl." They may also imitate the way family members communicate by using the same gestures and words. For example, a toddler will say, "Mommy bye-bye" after the father says, "Mommy went bye-bye."<ref>{{cite web |year=2012 |title=Foundation: Imitation: California infant/toddler learning & development foundations |publisher=California Department of Education |access-date=November 10, 2012 |url=http://www.cde.ca.gov/sp/cd/re/itf09cogdevfdimit.asp}}</ref>

Toddlers love to imitate their parents and help when they can; imitation helps toddlers learn, and through their experiences lasting impressions are made. 12- to 36-month-olds learn by doing, not by watching, and so it is often recommended to be a good role model and caretaker by showing them simple tasks like putting on socks or holding a spoon.<ref>{{cite web |last=Stiefel |first=Chana |year=2012 |title=What Your Child Learns By Imitating You |publisher=Parents Magazine |url=http://www.parents.com/toddlers-preschoolers/development/behavioral/learning-by-imitating-you/}}</ref>

[[Duke University|Duke]] developmental psychologist Carol Eckerman did a study on toddlers imitating toddlers and found that at the age of 2 children involve themselves in imitation play to communicate with one another. This can be seen within a culture or across different cultures. 3 common imitative patterns Eckerman found were reciprocal imitation, follow-the-leader, and lead-follow.<ref>{{cite web |last=Malcom |first=Kelly |date=30 June 2000 |title=Studies shed light on toddler development |url=http://today.duke.edu/2000/06/toddler630.html}}</ref>

[[Kenneth Kaye]]'s "apprenticeship" theory of imitation rejected assumptions that other authors had made about its development. His research showed that there is no one simple imitation skill with its own course of development. What changes is the type of behavior imitated.<ref>{{cite book |first=Kenneth |last=Kaye |year=1982 |title=The Mental and Social Life of Babies |chapter=Imitation |pages=[https://archive.org/details/mentalsociallife0000kaye_a5t8/page/155 155–88] |isbn=978-0-226-42848-2 |title-link=The Mental and Social Life of Babies |publisher=University of Chicago Press }}</ref>

An important agenda for infancy is the progressive imitation of higher levels of use of signs, until the ultimate achievement of symbols. The principal role played by parents in this process is their provision of salient models within the facilitating frames that channel the infant's attention and organize his imitative efforts.

===Gender and age differences===
[[File:Matera, Italy. Still unaware of despair, a small boy of Matera, Italy, unconsciously repeats the gesture of his... - NARA - 541739.tif|thumb|left|A small boy of [[Matera]], [[Italy]], unconsciously repeats the gesture of his grandmother's hands, ca. 1948 – ca. 1955]]

Imitation and imitative behaviors do not manifest ubiquitously and evenly in all human individuals; some individuals rely more on imitated information than others.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Toyokawa|first1=Wataru|last2=Saito|first2=Yoshimatsu|last3=Kameda|first3=Tatsuya|date=2017-05-01|title=Individual differences in learning behaviours in humans: Asocial exploration tendency does not predict reliance on social learning|url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1090513816301180|journal=Evolution and Human Behavior|language=en|volume=38|issue=3|pages=325–333|doi=10.1016/j.evolhumbehav.2016.11.001|bibcode=2017EHumB..38..325T |issn=1090-5138}}</ref> Although imitation is very useful when it comes to cognitive learning with toddlers, research has shown that there are some gender and age differences when it comes to imitation. Research done to judge imitation in toddlers 2–3 years old shows that when faced with certain conditions "2-year-olds displayed more motor imitation than 3-year-olds, and 3-year-olds displayed more verbal-reality imitation than 2-year-olds. Boys displayed more motor imitation than girls."<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1080/00221325.1985.10532470 |title=Imitation in Toddlers as a Function of Motor and Verbal Aspects of Modeling |year=1985 |last1=Patrick |first1=Karen |last2=Richman |first2=Charles L. |journal=The Journal of Genetic Psychology |volume=146 |issue=4 |pages=507–18 |pmid=3835229}}</ref>

No other research is more controversial pertaining gender differences in toddler imitation than psychologist, Bandura's, [[bobo doll experiment]]s.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://psychology.about.com/od/classicpsychologystudies/a/bobo-doll-experiment.htm |first=Kendra |last=Cherry |title=Bobo Doll Experiment |work=About.com Health |publisher=[[About.com]] |access-date=November 20, 2012 |archive-date=March 5, 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160305013101/http://psychology.about.com/od/classicpsychologystudies/a/bobo-doll-experiment.htm }}</ref> The goal of the experiment was to see what happens to toddlers when exposed to aggressive and non-aggressive adults, would the toddlers imitate the behavior of the adults and if so, which gender is more likely to imitate the aggressive adult. In the beginning of the experiment Bandura had several predictions that actually came true. Children exposed to violent adults will imitate the actions of that adult when the adult is not present, boys who had observed an adult of the opposite sex act aggressively are less likely to act violently than those who witnessed a male adult act violently. In fact "boys who observed an adult male behaving violently were more influenced than those who had observed a female model behavior aggressively". One observation was that while boys are likely to imitate physical acts of violence, girls are likely to imitate verbal acts of violence.

===Negative imitation===
Imitation plays a major role on how a toddler interprets the world. Much of a child's understanding is derived from imitation, due to a lack of verbal skill imitation in toddlers for communication.{{Citation needed|date=April 2013}} It is what connects them to the communicating world, as they continue to grow they begin to learn more. This may mean that it is crucial for parents to be cautious as to how they act and behave around their toddlers. Imitation is the toddlers way of confirming and dis-conforming socially acceptable actions in society. Actions like washing dishes, cleaning up the house and doing chores are actions you want your toddlers to imitate. Imitating negative things is something that is never beyond young toddlers. If they are exposed to cursing and violence, it is going to be what the child views as the norm of their world, since imitation is the "mental activity that helps to formulate the conceptions of the world for toddlers".<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Hay |first1=Dennis C. |last2=Young |first2=Andrew W. |last3=Ellis |first3=Andrew W. |date=November 1991 |title=Routes through the Face Recognition System |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1080/14640749108400957 |journal=The Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology Section A |language=en |volume=43 |issue=4 |pages=761–791 |doi=10.1080/14640749108400957 |pmid=1775665 |s2cid=36149305 |issn=0272-4987|url-access=subscription }}</ref> So it is important for parents to be careful what they say or do in front of their children.{{Citation needed|date=April 2013}}

===Autism===
Children with autism exhibit significant impairment in imitation skills.<ref name="Ingersoll, B. 2008" /> Imitation deficits have been reported on a variety of tasks including symbolic and non-symbolic body movements, symbolic and functional object use, vocalizations, and facial expressions.<ref name="Ingersoll, B. 2008" /> In contrast, typically-developing children can copy a broad range of novel (as well as familiar) rules from a very early age.<ref name = Subiaul>{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/j.cogdev.2006.10.003 |title=Cognitive imitation in typically-developing 3- and 4-year olds and individuals with autism |year=2007 |last1=Subiaul |first1=Francys |last2=Lurie |first2=Herbert |last3=Romansky |first3=Kathryn |last4=Klein |first4=Tovah |last5=Holmes |first5=David |last6=Terrace |first6=Herbert |journal=Cognitive Development |volume=22 |issue=2 |page=230 |pmid=20148122 |pmc=2818295|bibcode=1996CogD...11..491S }}</ref> Problems with imitation discriminate children with autism from those with other developmental disorders as early as age 2 and continue into [[adulthood]].<ref name = Rogers>{{cite journal |doi=10.1111/1469-7610.00162 |title=Imitation performance in toddlers with autism and those with other developmental disorders |year=2003 |last1=Rogers |first1=Sally J. |last2=Hepburn |first2=Susan L. |last3=Stackhouse |first3=Tracy |last4=Wehner |first4=Elizabeth |journal=Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry |volume=44 |issue=5 |pages=763–81 |pmid=12831120|s2cid=17936792 }}</ref>

Children with autism exhibit significant deficits in imitation that are associated with impairments in other social communication skills. It is unclear whether imitation is mediating these relationships directly, or whether they are due to some other developmental variable that is also reflected in the measurement of imitation skills.<ref name="Ingersoll, B. 2008" />

On the contrary, research from the early 21st century suggests that people affected with forms of [[high-functioning autism]] easily interact with one another by using a more analytically-centered communication approach rather than an imitative [[Social cue|cue-based]] approach,<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Crompton|first1=Catherine J.|last2=Sharp|first2=Martha|last3=Axbey|first3=Harriet|last4=Fletcher-Watson|first4=Sue|last5=Flynn|first5=Emma G.|last6=Ropar|first6=Danielle|date=2020|title=Neurotype-Matching, but Not Being Autistic, Influences Self and Observer Ratings of Interpersonal Rapport|journal=Frontiers in Psychology|volume=11|page=2961|article-number=586171 |doi=10.3389/fpsyg.2020.586171|issn=1664-1078|pmc=7645034|pmid=33192918|doi-access=free}}</ref> suggesting that reduced imitative capabilities do not affect abilities for expressive social behavior but only the understanding of said social behavior. Social communication is not negatively affected when said communication involves less or no imitation. Children with autism may have significant problems understanding typical social communication not because of inherent social deficits, but because of [[Double empathy problem|differences in communication style]] which affect [[Reciprocity (social psychology)|reciprocal]] understanding.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Milton|first=Damian E.M.|author-link = Damian Milton |date=2012-10-01|title=On the ontological status of autism: the 'double empathy problem'|journal=Disability & Society|volume=27|issue=6|pages=883–887|doi=10.1080/09687599.2012.710008|s2cid=54047060|issn=0968-7599}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last=DeThorne|first=Laura S.|date=2020-03-01|title=Revealing the Double Empathy Problem|journal=The ASHA Leader|volume=25|issue=3|pages=58–65|language=EN|doi=10.1044/leader.ftr2.25042020.58|s2cid=216359201|doi-access=free}}</ref>

Autistic individuals are also shown to possess increased [[Analytic reasoning|analytical]], [[Cognition|cognitive]], and [[visual processing]],<ref>{{Cite journal|title=Enhanced visual processing contributes to matrix reasoning in autism|year=2009|doi=10.1002/hbm.20831|pmc=2787806|pmid=19530215|last1=Soulières|first1=Isabelle|last2=Dawson|first2=Michelle|last3=Samson|first3=Fabienne|last4=Barbeau|first4=Elise B.|last5=Sahyoun|first5=Chérif P.|last6=Strangman|first6=Gary E.|last7=Zeffiro|first7=Thomas A.|last8=Mottron|first8=Laurent|journal=Human Brain Mapping|volume=30|issue=12|pages=4082–4107}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Plaisted|first1=Kate|last2=O'Riordan|first2=Michelle|last3=Baron-Cohen|first3=Simon|date=July 1998|title=Enhanced Discrimination of Novel, Highly Similar Stimuli by Adults with Autism During a Perceptual Learning Task|journal=Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry|volume=39|issue=5|pages=765–775|doi=10.1017/s0021963098002601|doi-broken-date=January 22, 2026 |pmid=9690939|issn=0021-9630}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|last1=O'Riordan|first1=Michelle|last2=Plaisted|first2=Kate|date=2001-11-01|title=Enhanced discrimination in autism|journal=The Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology Section A|language=en|volume=54|issue=4|pages=961–979|doi=10.1080/713756000|pmid=11765744|s2cid=144120552|issn=0272-4987}}</ref> suggesting that they have no true impairments in observing the actions of others but may decide not to imitate them because they do not analytically understand them.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Baron-Cohen|first1=Simon|last2=Belmonte|first2=Matthew K.|date=2005-07-21|title=AUTISM: A Window Onto the Development of the Social and the Analytic Brain|url=https://www.annualreviews.org/doi/10.1146/annurev.neuro.27.070203.144137|journal=Annual Review of Neuroscience|volume=28|issue=1|pages=109–126|doi=10.1146/annurev.neuro.27.070203.144137|pmid=16033325|issn=0147-006X|url-access=subscription}}</ref> A 2016 study has shown that involuntary, spontaneous facial [[mimicry]] – which supposedly depends on the mirror neuron system – is intact in individuals with autism, contrasting with previous studies and suggesting that the mirror neuron system is not inherently broken in autistic individuals.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Schulte-Rüther |first1=Martin |last2=Otte |first2=Ellen |last3=Adigüzel |first3=Kübra |last4=Firk |first4=Christine |last5=Herpertz-Dahlmann |first5=Beate |last6=Koch |first6=Iring |last7=Konrad |first7=Kerstin |date=2016-06-28 |title=Intact mirror mechanisms for automatic facial emotions in children and adolescents with autism spectrum disorder: Intact mirror mechanisms in Austism |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/aur.1654 |journal=Autism Research |language=en |volume=10 |issue=2 |pages=298–310 |doi=10.1002/aur.1654|pmid=27349835 |s2cid=4924514 |url-access=subscription }}</ref>

==Automatic imitation==
The automatic imitation comes very fast when a [[Stimulus (physiology)|stimulus]] is given to replicate. The imitation can match the commands with the visual stimulus (compatible) or it cannot match the commands with the visual stimulus (incompatible). For example: '[[Simon Says]]', a game played with children where they are told to follow the commands given by the adult. In this game, the adult gives the commands and shows the actions; the commands given can either match the action to be done or it will not match the action. The children who imitate the adult who has given the command with the correct action will stay in the game. The children who imitate the command with the wrong action will go out of the game, and this is where the child's automatic imitation comes into play. Psychologically, the visual stimulus being looked upon by the child is being imitated faster than the imitation of the command. In addition, the response times were faster in compatible scenarios than in incompatible scenarios.<ref>{{cite journal |doi=10.1037/a0022288 |title=Automatic imitation |year=2011 |last1=Heyes |first1=Cecilia |journal=Psychological Bulletin |volume=137 |issue=3 |pages=463–83 |pmid=21280938|s2cid=6975248 }}</ref>

Children are surrounded by many different people, day by day. Their parents make a big impact on them, and usually what the children do is what they have seen their parent do. In this article they found that a child, simply watching its mother sweep the floor, right after soon picks up on it and starts to imitate the mother by sweeping the floor. By the children imitating, they are really teaching themselves how to do things without instruction from the parent or guardian. Toddlers love to play the game of house. They picked up on this game of house by television, school or at home; they play the game how they see it. The kids imitate their parents or anybody in their family. In the article it says it is so easy for them to pick up on the things they see on an everyday basis.{{Citation needed|date=April 2013}}

==Over-imitation==
Over-imitation is "the tendency of young children to copy all of an adult model's actions, even components that are irrelevant for the task at hand."<ref>{{Cite book|title=Navigating the Social World|last1=Lyons|first1=Derek E.|last2=Keil|first2=Frank C.|date=2013-04-23|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-989071-2|pages=145–149|language=en-US|doi=10.1093/acprof:oso/9780199890712.003.0026}}</ref> According to this human and cross-cultural phenomenon, a child has a strong tendency to automatically encode the deliberate action of an adult as causally meaningful even when the child observes evidence that proves that its performance is unnecessary. It is suggested that over-imitation "may be critical to the transmission of human culture." Experiments done by Lyons et al. (2007) has shown that when there are obvious pedagogical cues, children tend to imitate step by step, including many unnecessary steps; without pedagogical cues, children will simply skip those useless steps.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Lyons |first1=Derek E. |last2=Young |first2=Andrew G. |last3=Keil |first3=Frank C. |date=2007-12-11 |title=The hidden structure of overimitation |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |language=en |volume=104 |issue=50 |pages=19751–19756 |doi=10.1073/pnas.0704452104 |issn=0027-8424 |pmc=2148370 |pmid=18056814|bibcode=2007PNAS..10419751L |doi-access=free }}</ref>

However, another study suggests that children do not just "blindly follow the crowd" since they can also be just as discriminating as adults in choosing whether an unnecessary action should be copied or not.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.sciencenews.org/blog/growth-curve/kids-are-selective-imitators-not-extreme-copycats?tgt=nr|title=Kids are selective imitators, not extreme copycats|last=Bower|first=Bruce|website=Science News|language=en|access-date=2018-05-18|date=2018-05-15}}</ref> They may imitate additional but unnecessary steps to a novel process if the adult demonstrations are all the same. However, in cases where one out of four adults showed a better technique, only 40% actually copied the extra step, as described by Evans, Carpenter and others.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Evans|first1=Cara L.|last2=Laland|first2=Kevin N.|last3=Carpenter|first3=Malinda|last4=Kendal|first4=Rachel L.|date=2017-12-17|title=Selective copying of the majority suggests children are broadly "optimal-" rather than "over-" imitators|journal=Developmental Science|volume=6|issue=1|language=en|article-number=e12637|doi=10.1111/desc.12637|pmid=29250871|issn=1363-755X|url=http://dro.dur.ac.uk/23137/1/23137.pdf|hdl=10023/16702}}</ref> Children's imitation is selective, also known as "selective imitation". Studies have shown that children tend to imitate older, competitive, and trustworthy individuals.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Koenig |first1=M. A. |last2=Clement |first2=F. |last3=Harris |first3=P. L. |date=2004-10-01 |title=Trust in Testimony: Children's Use of True and False Statements |url=http://pss.sagepub.com/lookup/doi/10.1111/j.0956-7976.2004.00742.x |journal=Psychological Science |language=en |volume=15 |issue=10 |pages=694–698 |doi=10.1111/j.0956-7976.2004.00742.x |pmid=15447641 |s2cid=2116025 |issn=0956-7976|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

==Deferred imitation==
Piaget coined the term ''deferred imitation'' and suggested that it arises out of the child's increasing ability to "form mental representations of behavior performed by others."<ref name="Carlson" /> Deferred imitation is also "the ability to reproduce a previously witnessed action or sequence of actions in the absence of current perceptual support for the action."<ref name="Hopper, L. M. 2010" /> Instead of copying what is currently occurring, individuals repeat the action or behavior later on. It appears that infants show an improving ability for deferred imitation as they get older, especially by 24 months. By 24 months, infants are able to imitate action sequences after a delay of up to three months, meaning that "they're able to generalize knowledge they have gained from one test environment to another and from one test object to another."<ref name="Hopper, L. M. 2010" />

A child's deferred imitation ability "to form mental representations of actions occurring in everyday life and their knowledge of communicative gestures" has also been linked to earlier productive language development.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Sundqvist |first1=Annette |last2=Nordqvist |first2=Emelie |last3=Koch |first3=Felix-Sebastian |last4=Heimann |first4=Mikael |title=Early declarative memory predicts productive language: A longitudinal study of deferred imitation and communication at 9 and 16months |journal=Journal of Experimental Child Psychology |date=November 2016 |volume=151 |pages=109–119 |doi=10.1016/j.jecp.2016.01.015 |pmid=26925719 |doi-access=free }}</ref> Between 9 (preverbal period) and 16 months (verbal period), deferred imitation performance on a standard actions-on-objects task was consistent in one longitudinal study testing participants' ability to complete a target action, with high achievers at 9 months remaining so at 16 months. Gestural development at 9 months was also linked to productive language at 16 months. Researchers now believe that early deferred imitation ability is indicative of early declarative memory, also considered a predictor of productive language development.
